elements.sent-2411-001: We present a novel approach for calculating the gravitational self-force (GSF) in the Lorenz gauge, employing hyperboloidal slicing and spectral methods.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our method builds on the previous work that applied hyperboloidal surfaces and spectral approaches to scalar-field toy model~[Phys.. Rev.
elements.sent-2411-003: D 105, 104033 (2022)], extending them to handle gravitational perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-004: Focusing on first-order metric perturbations, we address the construction of the hyperboloidal foliation, detailing the minimal gauge choice.
elements.sent-2411-005: The Lorenz gauge is adopted to facilitate well-understood regularisation procedures, which are essential for obtaining physically meaningful GSF results.
elements.sent-2411-006: We calculate of the Lorenz gauge metric perturbation via a (known) gauge transformation from the Regge-Wheeler gauge.
elements.sent-2411-007: Our approach yields a robust framework for obtaining the metric perturbation components needed to calculate key physical quantities, such as radiative fluxes, the Detweiler redshift, and self-force corrections.
elements.sent-2411-008: Furthermore, the compactified hyperboloidal approach allows us to efficiently calculate the metric perturbation throughout the entire spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-009: This work thus establishes a foundational methodology for future second-order GSF calculations within this gauge, offering computational efficiencies through spectral methods.
elements.sent-2411-010: Observations of compact binary coalescences from black holes and black holes and neutron stars are now a routine occurence within the field of gravitational wave astronomy.
elements.sent-2411-011: To date, the LIGO-Virgo-Kagra (LVK) collaboration \cite{LIGOScientific:2007fwp, LIGOScientific:2014pky, LIGOScientific:2014qfs, PhysRevD.102.062003, PhysRevLett.123.231107, VIRGO:2012dcp, VIRGO:2014yos, PhysRevLett.123.231108, 10.1093/ptep/ptaa125, PhysRevD.88.043007, Somiya:2011np has detected nearly 100 compact binary coalescences, with the majority of these detections being binary black hole mergers.}
elements.sent-2411-012: As our ground based detectors have improved over the course of the LVK observing runs, we have seen glimpses of compact binary coalescences with greater mass asymmetry \cite{LIGOScientific:2020stg, LIGOScientific:2020zkf between the two consitutents.}
elements.sent-2411-013: This trend is expected to continue with the current (O4) and planned observing runs as the number of gravitational wave (GW) detections increase.
elements.sent-2411-014: Beyond our current range of ground based detectors, we shall soon be able to detect a lower range of frequencies of GW with new observatories—including next-generation (XG) ground-based detectors like the Einstein Telescope \cite{Punturo:2010zz and Cosmic Explorer \cite{Reitze:2019iox, Evans:2021gyd , and the space-based LISA mission \cite{LISA:2017pwj .}}}
elements.sent-2411-015: We expect this millihertz frequency regime will be populated by the most asymmetric compact binaries in nature: intermediate-mass-ratio inspirals (IMRIs) and extreme-mass-ratio inspirals (EMRIs).
elements.sent-2411-016: EMRIs in particular, are systems characterised by a mass-ratio $\epsilon := \mu / M \lesssim - 10^{-4}$, where $M$ and $\mu$ are the masses of primary and secondary bodies respectively.
elements.sent-2411-017: The task of modelling such systems is most naturally done through gravitational self-force (GSF) theory.
elements.sent-2411-018: GSF theory is based upon black hole perturbation theory (BHPT), where the smaller of the two objects is treated as a perturber within the stationary, background spacetime of the primary black hole.
elements.sent-2411-019: In such binaries, the disparate masses lead to asymmetric time-scales of the differing aspects of the evolution: the orbital timescale $T_{\rm orb \sim M$ and the radiation-reaction timescale $T_{\rm rad \sim M / \epsilon$.}}
elements.sent-2411-020: For EMRIs, since $\epsilon \ll 1$, $T_{\rm orb \ll T_{\rm rad}$, and hence such binaries have a clear seperation of scales.}
elements.sent-2411-021: On the orbital timescale, the motion of the secondary closely resembles that of a geodesic in a background spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-022: Yet, over the course of the evolution, as we approach the radiation-reaction timescale, the secondary gradually deviates away from such geodesic motion due to the backreaction between the secondary and the gravitational peturbation it sources.
elements.sent-2411-023: This interaction can be thought of as gravitational ``self-force'' which accelerates the secondary away from geodesic motion in the background spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-024: The gravitational self-force up to second perturbative order will lead to a cumulative correction of ${\cal O (\epsilon^{0 )$ in the motion of the secondary on the radiation reaction timescale.}}
elements.sent-2411-025: More pertinently, it has been shown that corrections up to such perturbative order will contribute ${\cal O (\epsilon^{0 )$ \cite{Hinderer-Flanagan:08,Burke:2023lno to the accumulated phase of the gravitational waveform.}}}
elements.sent-2411-026: Therefore, if one is to accurately model EMRIs over the duration of an inspiral, one must consider an expansion of the metric up-to-and-including second-order in the mass ratio.
elements.sent-2411-027: To date, second-order calculations have addressed the binding energy \cite{Pound:2019lzj , energy flux \cite{Warburton:2021kwk , and gravitational waveform \cite{Wardell:2021fyy for a smaller spinning secondary \cite{Mathews:2021rod, MathewsCapra orbiting a Schwarzschild black hole, as well as for cases involving a slowly rotating black hole \cite{MathewsCapra .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-028: Such calculations leverage the disparity between the orbital and radiation-reaction timescales to construct the waveforms from two distinct components: (i) an offline calculation of the Fourier domain field equations on a grid of slow-evolving parameters and (ii) a quick (online) step that solves the simple ODEs on this grid.
elements.sent-2411-029: A recent development within this offline step is the use of hyperboloidal slicing to foliate the spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-030: Hyperboloidal foliation penertrates the black hole horizon and future null infinity directly, as opposed to the usual spacetime time coordinate, which will only intersect the bifurcation sphere $({\cal B )$ and spatial infinity $(i^{0 )$.}}
elements.sent-2411-031: Such a foliation is extremely advantageous when considering Fourier domain self-force calculations.
elements.sent-2411-032: For example, in Refs.~\cite{Pound:2019lzj, Wardell:2021fyy , the use of hyperboloidal slicing is an important element to tame the nature of the source terms that appear in the multiscale expansion \cite{Miller:2020bft, Miller:2023ers .}}
elements.sent-2411-033: More recently, there has been a concerted effort to realise the full potential of hyperboloidal methods in BHPT \cite{Zenginoglu:2007jw, Zenginoglu:2011jz, PanossoMacedo:2018hab, PanossoMacedo:2019npm, PanossoMacedo:2024nkw and GSF calculations \cite{PanossoMacedo:2022fdi, PanossoMacedo:2024pox .}}
elements.sent-2411-034: This has culminated in Ref.~\cite{PanossoMacedo:2022fdi , hereafter known as Paper I, which presented a novel approach to self-force calculations that compactified the hyperboloidal surfaces, and solved the resulting Fourier domain problem on a full $(\ell, m)$ spherical decomposition with a spectral method.}
elements.sent-2411-035: Ref.~\cite{PanossoMacedo:2024pox , then built upon this method by introducing an alternative $m$-mode decomposition for the problem in attempt to overcome a major bottleneck in exisiting second-order calculations.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Paper I and Ref.~\cite{PanossoMacedo:2024pox , however, only applied this novel approach to a scalar field toy model for different scenarios that mimic the problems that the gravitational case faces.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In this work, we seek to extend this approach from a scalar toy model to gravitational perturbations.
elements.sent-2411-038: We present the first complete gravitational self-force calculation through hyperboloidal slicing and spectral methods.
elements.sent-2411-039: We shall conduct a calculation at first-order in the mass-ratio in the Lorenz gauge.
elements.sent-2411-040: The Lorenz gauge is particularly advantageous when considering GSF calculations for numerous reasons.
elements.sent-2411-041: Firstly, the GSF is most well-understood in this gauge, particularly the procedure of regularisation that is required to obtain physically meaningful results.
elements.sent-2411-042: In self-force calculations, the secondary body is “skeletonised” into a point-particle singularity, enabling the subtraction of the dominant singular component of the particle’s gravitational field.
elements.sent-2411-043: This approach isolates the regular residual part of the field, which physically contributes to the self-force.
elements.sent-2411-044: In the Lorenz gauge, this singularity is isotropic, and the procedures for regularising this singularity are well established.
elements.sent-2411-045: In other gauges, such as the radiation gauge, one encounters extended singularities that are more difficult to reconcile\footnote{An alternative scheme is avaliable to conduct self-force calculations within the radiation gauge that is free from such gauge singularites is the Green-Hollands-Zimmerman metric reconstruction scheme \cite{Toomani:2021jlo, Bourg:2024vre .. .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Furthermore, the asymptotic behaviour of the Lorenz gauge metric perturbation has been studied extensively, and is not divergent towards either null infinity and horizon.
elements.sent-2411-047: Another motivation for conducting our calculation within the Lorenz gauge is that it currently forms the basis of all GSF calculations at second perturbative order.
elements.sent-2411-048: It is therefore natural to conduct our calculation, albeit at first-order, within the same gauge.
elements.sent-2411-049: Eventually one envisions future second-order calculations being completely based along the methodology presented in this work, Paper I, and Ref.~\cite{PanossoMacedo:2024pox .}
elements.sent-2411-050: As such, the calculation here could serve as a key ingredient for future second-order calculations.
elements.sent-2411-051: This paper is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-052: We begin in \secref{hyperboloidal_slicing with a review of the construction of our hyperboloidal foliation and the specific choice of minimal gauge slicing.}
elements.sent-2411-053: While this follows the same construction presented in Paper I, we provide a more comprehensive description here.
elements.sent-2411-054: In \secref{lorenz_gauge and \secref{regge_wheeler_gauge , we outline the Lorenz and Regge-Wheeler gauges within the context of GSF calculations.}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The gauge transformation between them is reviewed in \secref{gauge_transformations .}
elements.sent-2411-056: Our hyperboloidal method for solving the necessary Regge-Wheeler(-Zerilli) fields to facilitate the transformation from the Regge-Wheeler gauge to the Lorenz gauge is presented in \secref{hyperboloidal_rwz_equation , and the gauge fields involved in this calculation are emphasised in \secref{gauge_fields .}}
elements.sent-2411-057: We then outline the techniques for full metric reconstruction (\secref{metric_reconstruction ), the calculation of fluxes (\secref{gravitational_wave_fluxes ), the gravitational self-force (\secref{self_force ), and the Detweiler redshift (\secref{detweiler_redshift ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: Finally, \secref{conclusion provides a summary of our results and a discussion of future research directions.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Additional details are presented in the Appendices.
elements.sent-2411-060: We begin by establishing the conventions used throughout this work.
elements.sent-2411-061: Geometrised units are employed, setting $G = c = 1$, and the metric signature is taken to be $(- + + +)$.
elements.sent-2411-062: We employ Schwarzschild coordinates $(t, r, \theta, \varphi)$ with the usual metric function $f(r) = 1 - 2M / r$, where $M$ is the mass of the primary.
elements.sent-2411-063: The standard line element for the Schwarzschild solution in these coordinates then reads \beq ds^{2 = -f(r)^{2 dt^{2 + f(r)^{-1 dr^{2 + r^{2 (d\theta^{2 + d\varphi^{2 ).. \eeq.}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-064: We shall introduce compact, horizon-penetrating hyperboloidal coordinates $(\tau, \sigma, \theta, \varphi)$ through the use of scri-fixing and the height-function technique.
elements.sent-2411-065: To constuct such coordinates, we follow the approach of Refs.~\cite{PanossoMacedo:2018hab, PanossoMacedo:2019npm by first considering horizon-penertrating ingoing Eddington-Finkelstein coordinates $(v, r, \theta, \varphi)$, where $v = t + \rstar(r)$, that then can be suitably augmented to that reach both the horizon and null infinity.}
elements.sent-2411-066: Here we have first introduced the tortoise coordinate, which is defined by the relation $d\rstar/dr = f(r)^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-067: Rewriting the line element from \eqn{schwarzschild_line_element in terms of these coordinates one finds the usual form \beq ds^{2 = -f(r)dv^{2 + 2 dv dr + r^{2 (d\theta^{2 + d\varphi^{2 ).}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: However, the hypersurface where $\rstar \rightarrow \infty$ (along $v = \text{const}$) is in fact at past null infinity, $\scri^{-}$.
elements.sent-2411-070: One can fix this freedom when one switches to our hyperboloidal foliation.
elements.sent-2411-071: We now introduce the hyperboloidal coordinates $(\tau, \sigma, \theta, \varphi)$ via the transformation from the horizon-penertrating ingoing Eddington-Finkelstein coordinates \beq v = \lambda \big( \tau - h(\sigma) \big),\quad\ r = \lambda\frac{\rho(\sigma) {\sigma .}}
elements.sent-2411-072: Since our spacetime is spherically symmetric, the radial function, $\rho(\sigma)$, is the areal radius on the conformal representation of the spacetime whilst the conformal factor, identified in terms $\sigma$, is given by \beq \Omega = \frac{\sigma {\lambda .}}
elements.sent-2411-073: The function, $h(\sigma)$, is then specified to ensure the hyperboloidal slices foliates future null infinity $\scri^{+}$.
elements.sent-2411-074: To find the appropriate form for $h(\sigma)$, one can consider the the ingoing and outgoing null vectors $\tilde{k ^{\alpha}$ and $\tilde{l ^{\alpha}$ in the conformal spacetime, as in Refs.~\cite{PanossoMacedo:2018hab, PanossoMacedo:2019npm .}}}
elements.sent-2411-075: The null vectors are normalised as $\tilde{g _{\alpha\beta}\tilde{l ^{\alpha}\tilde{k ^{\beta = -1$, and are related to the null vectors in Schwarzschild spacetime by $\tilde{l ^{\alpha = \Omega^{-1 l^{\alpha}$ and $\tilde{k ^{\alpha = \Omega^{-1 k^{\alpha}$.}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-076: In contrast to the null vectors appearing in Eqs.~(\ref{eq:outgoing_null_vector_eddington_finklestein ) -(\ref{eq:ingoing_null_vector_eddington_finklestein ), the hyperboloidal surfaces parametrised with a height function as in \eqn{hyperboloidal_transformation_ingoing_form do reach $\scri^{+}$ at $\sigma = 0$ if $\tau$ satisfies \beq \tilde{k ^{\alpha}\partial_{\alpha}\tau = 1.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-077: As in \cite{PanossoMacedo:2018hab , one can assume that since $\rho(\sigma)$ is the areal radius of the conformal spacetime it is a regular function on its domain that yields non-vanishing, positive values.}
elements.sent-2411-078: This is important when considering the phase shift, $\beta(\sigma)$, that appears in the outgoing null vector in \eqn{null_vector_hyperboloidal .}
elements.sent-2411-079: If you assume this phase shift has the generic form of an expansion of $\sigma$, \beq \beta(\sigma) = \beta_{0 + \beta_{1}\sigma + {\cal O (\sigma^{2 ), \eeq then this will yield \beq \rho(\sigma) = \beta_{0 + \rho_{1}\sigma - \beta_{1}\sigma \ln\sigma.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: Identifying $\beta_{0 = \rho_{0}$ for notational simplicity and inserting the result for $\rho(\sigma)$ into \eqn{hyperboloidal_transformation_ingoing_form , we find \beq F(\sigma) = 1 - \frac{2M {\lambda\rho_{0 }\sigma + {\cal O (\sigma^{2 ), \eeq and \beq h^{\prime (\sigma) = \frac{2\rho_{0 {\sigma^{2 \left[ 1 + \frac{2M {\lambda\rho_{0 }\sigma \right] + {\cal O (\sigma^{0 ).}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-081: Now we have appropiately behaved (conformal) null vectors, we are free to integrate \eqn{height_function_derivative to give \beq h(\sigma) = h_{0 (\sigma) + A(\sigma), \eeq where \beq h_{0 (\sigma) := -2\rho_{0 \left[ \frac{1 {\sigma - \frac{2M {\lambda\rho_{0 \ln \sigma \right].}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-082: In turn, this imposes the following restriction on the derivative of the height function \cite{PanossoMacedo:2018hab : \beq 0 < \sigma^{2 h^{\prime (\sigma) < \frac{2\beta(\sigma) {F(\sigma) .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: Specifically, $\beta(\sigma)$ represents the freedom to choose the conformal representation of the spacetime, while $A(\sigma)$ characterises the foliation.
elements.sent-2411-084: Notably, there is significant freedom in the choice of $A(\sigma)$.
elements.sent-2411-085: For example, letting the function have an angular dependence, i.e. $A = A(\sigma, \theta)$, will allow one to construct a framework applicable for a background Kerr geometry \cite{PanossoMacedo:2019npm .}
elements.sent-2411-086: Since we now have completely identified the gauge degrees of freedom, we shall now discuss the main choice of gauge when applying hyperboloidal formulations in this work.
elements.sent-2411-087: The minimal gauge \cite{PanossoMacedo:2018hab, PanossoMacedo:2019npm , assumes the gauge functions $\beta(\sigma)$ and $A(\sigma)$ take their simplest form, whereby \beq \beta(\sigma) = \rho_{0 ,\quad\quad A(\sigma) = 0.}}
elements.sent-2411-088: In fact, one can go further and demand that the location of the event horizon within the compactified regime is at $\sigma_{H = 1$.}
elements.sent-2411-089: This choice is useful for numerical purposes and also contrains the parameters such that \beq \rho_{0 = \frac{2M {\lambda - \rho_{1 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-091: This minimal structure for the hyperboloidal hypersufaces are demonstrated in the Penrose Diagram shown in \fig{minimal_gauge_penrose .}
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\columnwidth]{PenroseDiagram.pdf} \caption{Penrose diagram for the Schwarzschild exterior spacetime region with minimal gauge hyperboloidal slices. The solid blue curves depict hyperboloidal time surfaces $\tau-$constant extending between the black-hole horizon ${\cal H}^+$ at $\sigma=1$ and future null infinity ${\scri^+}$ as $\sigma=0$. Here, we set $\lambda = 4M$ and $\rho_{0} = 1/2$ to align with the exact minimal gauge slicing adopted in this work. These coordinates provide a smooth foliation on the full exterior spacetime, which means that both the horizon and null infinity can be included in the computational domain. One should note our hyperboloidal slices are spacelike everywhere, and therefore identical to the null vectors exactly at ${\scri^+}$. The hypersufaces might appear to be tangential to this surface is a result of the compactification of our spacetime by the mapping of the Penrose diagram.} \label{fig:minimal_gauge_penrose}

elements.sent-2411-133: Thus far, we have outlined an appropiate framework for solving the Lorenz-gauge field equations in the Fourier domain, which has been implemented in Refs.~\cite{Akcay:2010dx, Akcay:2013wfa, Wardell:2015ada, Miller:2023ers .}
elements.sent-2411-134: This implementation has involved the traditional approach of solving the individual ODEs through the method of variation of parameters.
elements.sent-2411-135: In this method, one first solves for a basis of homogeneous solutions, comprised of ``inner'' and ``outer'' solutions, that are regular at the horizon and at infinity, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-136: This basis of solutions is then used to construct appropiate weighting coefficients by convolving the homogeneous solutions against the source.
elements.sent-2411-137: It is these weighting coefficients, together with the homogeneous solutions, that are then used to construct the full, inhomogeneous solutions.
elements.sent-2411-138: This approach has been immensely successful, leading to the first second-order computations in the Lorenz gauge \cite{Pound:2020, Warburton:2021kwk, Wardell:2021fyy .}
elements.sent-2411-139: However, it is not without its drawbacks.
elements.sent-2411-140: Fundamentally, this methodology is not directly applicable when the background spacetime is a Kerr black hole.
elements.sent-2411-141: In Kerr spacetime, there is no known ansatz to reduce the partial differential equations to a set of decoupled ordinary differential equations.
elements.sent-2411-142: Therefore one must consider an alternative formulation if one would like to solve for the Lorenz gauge metric peturbation in a Kerr spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-143: One such approach, outlined in \cite{Dolan:2023enf, Wardell:2024yoi , is to construct a full, Lorenz gauge metric perturbation from a linear combination of differential operators acting on solutions to the Teukolsky equation and related scalars, which are all solutions found through ODEs in the Fourier domain.}
elements.sent-2411-144: The analogue of this approach in the case of Schwarzschild background is to construct the metric perturbation from the Regge-Wheeler-Zerilli functions through an appropiate gauge transformation of the metric perturbation from Regge-Wheeler gauge to Lorenz gauge.
elements.sent-2411-145: This approach was outlined independently by Berndtson in \cite{Berndtson:2007gsc and Hopper and Evans\footnote{The transformation outlined in \cite{Hopper:2012ty is only given for the odd-parity sector, but the transformation is equivalent to the one presented by Berndtson in \cite{Berndtson:2007gsc .. in \cite{Hopper:2012ty .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-146: This approach has seperately been implemented by Durkan and Warburton in \cite{Durkan:2022fvm in the context of solving for the slow-evolution of the metric perturbation in Lorenz gauge, a crucial component of the multiscale expansion for second-order calculations.}
elements.sent-2411-147: From a computational perspective, the method of variation of parameters has significant limitations.
elements.sent-2411-148: Firstly, the homogeneous solutions are usually computed on a finite numerical grid using either asymptotic or Frobenius expansions.
elements.sent-2411-149: These boundary conditions, which are inconvenient to derive, can suffer from convergence problems if evaluated outside the “wave-zone”.
elements.sent-2411-150: A heuristic guide is that the wave-zone scales as $\sim1/\omega$ away from the source, and the boundary conditions should be evaluated at least this far to achieve convergence.
elements.sent-2411-151: For low-frequency radiative modes, this occurs at a very large radius, increasing the computational burden since the integration of the homogeneous solutions must be carried out over this entire domain, accumulating substantial numerical error.
elements.sent-2411-152: Additionally, in the Lorenz gauge, the homogeneous solutions with retarded boundary conditions are not regular near the opposite boundary from where they are defined \cite{Akcay:2013wfa .}
elements.sent-2411-153: This requires integrating over the large numerical domain from the large outer radius, where the homogeneous solutions can span many orders of magnitude, leading to precision loss.
elements.sent-2411-154: Furthermore, from the gauge conditions in Eqs.~(\ref{eq:gauge_even1 )-(\ref{eq:gauge_odd ), it is evident that in the limit $\omega \rightarrow 0$, some of the Fourier modes of the metric perturbation become linearly dependent \cite{Akcay:2010dx .}}}
elements.sent-2411-155: These issues are problematic in a variation of parameters implementation in the Lorenz gauge, as the inhomogeneous solutions are typically constructed through (numerical) matrix inversion.
elements.sent-2411-156: Degeneracies and other such issues lead to ill-conditioned matrices with large condition numbers, resulting in significant numerical error.

elements.sent-2411-493: In this work, we presented a comprehensive framework for the calculation of the gravtitational self-force in the Lorenz gauge for a secondary particle in a circular orbit around a Schwarzschild black hole using hyperboloidal slicing and spectral methods.
elements.sent-2411-494: Building on the toy model introduced in Paper I, we extended these methods to solve for gravitational peturbations in the Lorenz gauge, marking a significant step forward in the application of hyperboloidal and spectral techniques to self-force calculations.
elements.sent-2411-495: By adopting hyperboloidal slicing, we circumvent the challenges inherent to the traditional methods of variation of parameters, which suffer from numerical error due to large intergration domains and degenerate boundary conditions, especially at low frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-496: Our numerical results demonstrated the power of the hyperboloidal-spectral approach.
elements.sent-2411-497: We performed GSF calculations for a range of circular orbits, finding excellent agreement with known analytical results for energy and angular momentum fluxes at null infinity and the black hole horizon.
elements.sent-2411-498: The use of hyperboloidal slicing facilitated accurate evaluation of these fluxes directly at the boundaries, avoiding the numerical difficulties associated with truncating the domain at finite radii.
elements.sent-2411-499: The spectral method provided rapid convergence across the entire domain, even for high spherical harmonic modes, which are particularly challenging for standard Fourier domain approaches.
elements.sent-2411-500: Furthermore, the accuracy of the method was preserved for both small- and large-radius orbits, confirming its robustness in diverse regimes.
elements.sent-2411-501: In particular, our results for gauge invariant quantities like the Detweiler redshift for orbits in the weak-field show that the challenges that were summounted in Paper I for the scalar-field case are equally applicable for the gravitational case.
elements.sent-2411-502: Compactification, coupled with the analytical mesh refinement procedure we introduced in Paper I, allowed for detailed comparison with post-Newtonian expressions and we observed agreement to a high level of precision between our results.
elements.sent-2411-503: One would expect such a methodology will continue to provide a valuable link between gravitational self-force calculations and post-Newtonian theory.
elements.sent-2411-504: Looking forward, this work opens several avenues for further research.
elements.sent-2411-505: The success of hyperboloidal slicing in a Schwarzschild background motivates the extension of these methods to Kerr spacetime, where the lack of separability in the field equations presents a formidable challenge.
elements.sent-2411-506: The adaptability of the hyperboloidal approach, for which the foliation in Kerr spacetime is already presented in \cite{PanossoMacedo:2019npm , offers a promising direction for overcoming the difficulties associated with mode coupling in the Kerr geometry via the methods presented by Dolan et al. in Refs.~\cite{Dolan:2023enf, Wardell:2024yoi .}}
elements.sent-2411-507: This approach to metric reconstruction, which has parallels to the Berndtson method utilised in this work, could produce highly accurate Lorenz gauge metric perturbations in Kerr spacetime.
elements.sent-2411-508: This is not only valuable at first-perturbative order, where there are preciously little calculations avaliable, but could an important input for future calculations at second-order.
elements.sent-2411-509: Second-order GSF calculations rely at some point on metric perturbations calculated in the Lorenz gauge and the ability of the methods presented here to produce accurate and regular perturbations throughout the entire spacetime could be beneficial to extending the fidelity of second-order calculations.
elements.sent-2411-510: The weak field regime, and comparisons with post-Newtonian theory, have so far proved difficult for existing second-order GSF implementations \cite{Warburton:2024xnr .}
elements.sent-2411-511: Finally, the methods presented here and in Paper I, demonstrate the accuracy and efficacy for GSF calculations for circular orbits.
elements.sent-2411-512: But one of the key challenges within the parameter space of EMRIs is the extension to eccentricity.
elements.sent-2411-513: EMRIs formed thorough formation channels like a dynamical capture \cite{Babak:2017tow , are expected to retain a siginifcant level of eccentricity in the LISA band.}
elements.sent-2411-514: Whilst there are existing eccentric Fourier-domain GSF calculations that leverage methods based on variation of parameters, our spectral method presented here would need considerable modifications to be used for secondaries on eccentric geodesics.
elements.sent-2411-515: This is because particle in our framework is no longer confined to a solitary value of $\rp$ for a given geodesic, but instead librates within a minimum and maximum radius.
elements.sent-2411-516: For a distributional or effective-source in the Fourier domain, this presents longstanding issues with Gibbs phenomenon.
elements.sent-2411-517: One could envisage a solution based on the methods presented in Ref.~\cite{Leather:2023dzj , but we shall leave this to future work.}

elements.sent-2411-538: In this section, we shall present the explicit analytical solutions for the static modes of the Lorenz gauge metric perturbation that have been derived previously in the literature.

elements.sent-2411-547: The generalised homogeneous RW equation with zero frequency can be written in terms of the compactified hyperboloidal coordinate $\sigma$ as \begin{equation}\sigma^{2}(1 - \sigma) \sdiff{\psi_{s}}{\sigma} + 2\sigma(1 - 3\sigma^{2})\diff{\psi_{s}}{\sigma} + \left( \ell (\ell + 1) + \sigma(1 - s^{2}) \right) \psi_{s} = 0. \label{eq:RWZ_eqn_zero_freq}\end{equation} \eqn{RWZ_eqn_zero_freq omits two independent homogeneous solutions, $\psi^{+ _{s}$ and $\psi^{- _{s}$, which are given by \begin{equation}\psi^{+}_{s}(\sigma) = \sigma^{\ell}\, _{2}F_{1}\left(1 + \ell - s, \ell + s + 1, 2(\ell + 1); \sigma \right) \psi^{-}_{s}(\sigma) = \sigma^{-\ell-1}\, _{2}F_{1}\left(- \ell - s, s - 1, -2\ell; \sigma\right),\end{equation} where $_{2 F_{1 (c_{1 , c_{2 , c_{3 ; x)$ is the Gauss hypergeometric function.}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-581: The final static component of the metric perturbation to compute is the monopole, or $(\ell, m) = (0, 0)$, piece.
elements.sent-2411-582: Up to a gauge piece, the monopole mode physically describes the variation in the mass of the background Schwarzschild geometry due to the presence of the secondary.
elements.sent-2411-583: This part of the perturbation can also be addressed analytically; however, it involves significantly more subtlety than the previous static cases.

elements.sent-2411-634: To provide a clear overview of the methodology presented in this work, we breifly outline the main steps involved in our calculation:.

elements.sent-2411-676: In this work, we presented a comprehensive framework for the calculation of the gravitational self-force in the Lorenz gauge for a secondary particle in a circular orbit around a Schwarzschild black hole using hyperboloidal slicing and spectral methods.
elements.sent-2411-677: Building on the toy model introduced in Paper I, we extended these methods to solve for gravitational perturbations in the Lorenz gauge, marking a significant step forward in the application of hyperboloidal and spectral techniques to self-force calculations.
elements.sent-2411-678: By adopting hyperboloidal slicing, we circumvent the challenges inherent to the traditional methods of variation of parameters, which suffer from numerical error due to large integration domains and degenerate boundary conditions, especially at low frequencies.
elements.sent-2411-679: In particular, our results for gauge invariant quantities like the Detweiler redshift for orbits in the weak-field show that the challenges that were surmounted in Paper I for the scalar-field case are equally applicable for the gravitational case.
elements.sent-2411-680: We also observe, in line with the findings of Paper I, that the hyperboloidal-spectral method offers a notable increase in efficiency compared to Fourier-domain approaches.
elements.sent-2411-681: Hyperboloidal slicing alleviates the need to resolve the highly oscillatory behaviour of the fields near null infinity and the black hole horizon, thereby reducing the resolution requirements and associated computational cost.
elements.sent-2411-682: This leads to significant reductions in runtime without any loss of precision.
elements.sent-2411-683: The radial profiles of the conformal fields remain smooth across the numerical domain, and the spherical harmonic modes exhibit more homogeneous structure.
elements.sent-2411-684: As a result, the spectral method achieves uniform and rapid convergence across all modes.
elements.sent-2411-685: Furthermore, since each mode is solved independently, the approach is inherently parallelisable and scales efficiently on modern computational architectures.
elements.sent-2411-686: This is not only valuable at first-perturbative order, where there are preciously little calculations avaliable \cite{Dolan:2023enf, Isoyama:2014mja , but could an important input for future calculations at second-order.}
